item.title: FOCUS I Coalitions, comprimise and collapse
item.description: IT was, by any measure, an improbable alliance. In June 1992, Mahendra Chaudhry, the man whose Finance Ministry Rabuka had been snatched at gunpoint five years earlier during the 1987 military coups — agreed to install his erstwhile tormentor as Prime Minister of Fiji. The arrangement was born not of goodwill but of pure arithmetic.
item.content:encoded:
IT was, by any measure, an improbable alliance. In June 1992, Mahendra Chaudhry, the man whose Finance Ministry Rabuka had been snatched at gunpoint five years earlier during the 1987 military coups — agreed to install his erstwhile tormentor as Prime Minister of Fiji.

The arrangement was born not of goodwill but of pure arithmetic.

Rabuka’s Soqosoqo ni Vakavulewa ni Taukei (SVT) had won only 30 of the 37 indigenous Fijian seats in the newly elected 70-seat Parliament, falling short of the outright majority required to govern.

Worse, a fierce internal challenge had emerged.

Josevata Kamikamica — the technocratic former finance minister backed by former prime minister Ratu Sir Kamisese Mara and the traditional eastern chiefly establishment — was threatening to split the indigenous vote on the parliamentary floor.

Kamikamica, to his proponents, was well educated, fiscally disciplined, and a product of the aristocratic civil service elite.

To the establishment, Rabuka was seen as an undisciplined commoner who had disrupted Fiji’s natural social order with his 1987 coups.

Faced with the prospect of losing the premiership to a rival within his own camp, Rabuka turned to the Fiji Labour Party and its 13 Indo-Fijian seats.

In exchange for its floor votes, Labour extracted a written agreement binding Rabuka to four concrete commitments: an immediate and comprehensive review of the discriminatory 1990 Constitution; a review of the Agricultural Landlord and Tenants Act (ALTA) to address expiring land leases; the revocation of anti-labour reforms enacted by the post-coup interim government; and the scrapping of Value Added Tax.

Chaudhry, ever the pragmatist, was asked by journalists why he was backing the man who had deposed him.

“I have noticed changes in him as he becomes more mature.”

It was a characteristically calculated answer.

The deal gave Rabuka his majority and it also gave Chaudhry leverage.

For twelve months, that leverage went largely unused.

Rabuka governed, but the four conditions gathered dust.

By mid-1993, Chaudhry’s patience had visibly expired.

In June of that year, Rabuka delivered a televised address announcing that the Great Council of Chiefs would need to be consulted before any constitutional changes could proceed.

“The process will take time,” Rabuka told the nation, insisting it was “far better and more helpful and productive to be patient.”

In a statement that same week, Chaudhry asked publicly: “Who is running this country — the GCC and the provincial councils, or elected representatives of the people? If so, then why are we going through this democratic charade of a parliamentary process?”

It was a declaration that the arrangement was on life support.

The constitutional question dominated the opening of the new parliamentary session.

President Ratu Sir Penaia Ganilau’s speech to the joint sitting of Parliament on June 21, 1993, sought to reassure, promising the review would be complete in time for the 1997 elections and that a successor to ALTA would be in place by 1995.

But within 24 hours, the deputy speaker had resigned.

Navin Maharaj, who also served as the FLP’s secretary-general, handed his letter to Speaker Dr Apenisa Kurisaqila, saying he could see “definitely clear indications” that the SVT had “no intention of having speedy discussions on a question of a review.”

He revealed that the FLP had written to Rabuka twice seeking a meeting and had received no acknowledgement.

On 23 June 1993, thirteen FLP members of parliament walked out of the chamber.

At a press conference afterwards, Chaudhry was blunt.

“I don’t trust him anymore,” he said of Rabuka.

“We gave him our conditional support, and we are entitled to withdraw that support. The whole process of reviewing the Constitution has been talked about for a year now and nothing has been done yet.”

He went further, accusing the Prime Minister of being controlled by external forces.

item.title: EMPOWERMENT I A young woman driven by compassion
item.description: SERVICE has become a defining thread in Malika Singh’s life, shaping both her career and her connection to the community. By profession, she is a nurse, caring for patients through some of their most difficult moments.
item.content:encoded:
SERVICE has become a defining thread in Malika Singh’s life, shaping both her career and her connection to the community.

By profession, she is a nurse, caring for patients through some of their most difficult moments.

Beyond the hospital walls, she is a dedicated volunteer and executive member of Vision Fiji, helping shape initiatives that strengthen communities and empower young people.

Her journey with the organisation began unexpectedly in 2020 when she was named a recipient of the Fiji Children’s Award.

At the time, she had never even heard of Vision Fiji.

“To be completely honest, I had not heard of Vision Fiji before that,” Malika said.

What began as a recognition of her community work would eventually become a lifelong connection, one that transformed her from an award recipient into a leader helping inspire the next generation of changemakers.

Long before she joined Vision Fiji, Malika had already discovered the value of service.

As a high school student, she and a group of friends established a youth organisation called Inari Fiji.

The group emerged from a simple but powerful desire – to help families struggling financially.

During a period marked by uncertainty and hardship, the young volunteers organised fundraising activities at flea markets, selling baked goods and second-hand clothing.

The proceeds were used to purchase groceries for vulnerable families identified through newspaper reports.

“We wanted to help families who were struggling financially,” she said.

“Whatever profit we made went towards purchasing groceries, which we packed into grocery packs and delivered to vulnerable families.”

Their work soon expanded beyond fundraising.

Recognising their own financial limitations as students, the group shifted towards projects that relied more on effort than money.

They established gardening initiatives at orphanages and hospitals, contributed school uniforms and bags to children in need, participated in beach clean-up campaigns and volunteered with animal shelters.

For Malika, these experiences became life-changing lessons in empathy and community responsibility.

At a time when many students were focused solely on academic success, some adults questioned why the young volunteers were investing so much time in community service.

“There was quite a bit of criticism from some adults who felt that we should be focusing solely on our studies,” Malika said.

Supported by a close-knit group of friends and parents who believed in their vision, she continued pursuing projects that brought practical assistance to those who needed it most.

Looking back, she credits those experiences with teaching her resilience and strengthening her commitment to service.

“If you have the heart to serve and make a difference, don’t let the opinions of others hold you back,” she said.

When Malika learned she had been selected for the Fiji Children’s Award, the news came as a complete surprise.

The award recognised her dedication to community service and the impact of the projects she had helped lead through Inari Fiji.

But for Malika, the recognition was about more than personal achievement.

“Although I had initiated many of the projects, none of it would have been possible without the support and dedication of my friends,” she said.

Receiving the award became a defining moment in her life.

“To be recognised as a child for trying to make a difference in your community is incredibly empowering.”

The experience left a lasting impression, planting what she describes as a “spark” that continues to guide her today.

item.title: WOMEN I A journey to the river
item.description: ALONG a dusty gravel road bordered by thick grass and tangled vines, five women from Nakavu Village in Namosi make their way toward a nearby creek. Bamboo fishing poles rest across their shoulders, their laughter carried through the cool midday air.
item.content:encoded:
ALONG a dusty gravel road bordered by thick grass and tangled vines, five women from Nakavu Village in Namosi make their way toward a nearby creek.

Bamboo fishing poles rest across their shoulders, their laughter carried through the cool midday air.

They have walked this road many times before, following a routine that has become part of life itself.

The sky hangs low with grey clouds, threatening rain, but that does little to dampen their spirits.

For these women, fishing is more than a pastime, it is a way to put food on the table, stretch household budgets and simply continue a tradition passed down through generations.

It is also a chance to spend time together, sharing stories, jokes and the latest happenings in the community.

As they reach the bank, the group spreads out to find suitable spots along the waterway.

The chatter gradually fades and is replaced by the gentle sound of flowing water.

One woman settles herself on a small patch of gravel jutting into the small creek, there she carefully lowers two bamboo poles into the water and fixes her eyes on the rippling surface.

Around her, the small creek moves steadily through a corridor of green vegetation. Thick vines drape over the banks while tall grasses bend gently in the breeze.

She watches the water with quiet concentration, waiting for the slightest movement that might signal a bite.

Beside her sits a sack holding supplies for the day, the simplicity of the scene reflects a way of life that remains deeply connected to nature.

Meanwhile, the others move between different spots along the creek.

For Lavenia Sovunivula, a fishing trip is about more than bringing food home to her family.

It is also a chance to spend time with friends, share laughter and continue a tradition that has long connected the women of Nakavu Village to the rivers and creeks around them.

The idea for the trip was made the day before.

Lavenia and five other women from the village agreed they would head out fishing together the next morning but before they could leave, there was work to be done at home.

“When we got up, we made sure everything was ready at home and lunches were prepared for the school children,” she said.

After completing their morning chores, the women turned their attention to preparing for the day ahead.

They dug up bait, gathered their fishing gear, showered and made their final preparations before making their way to catch the 9am bus.

Their destination was Wainikavika, one of the places where they often fish.

Armed with simple bamboo fishing poles and bait, the women travelled together, hoping for a productive day along the creek.

“The weather had changed, so we didn’t get a lot of catch at Wainikavika,” Lavenia said.

With little success, the group decided to return to the village earlier than expected.

Fishing trips like these are not a daily occurrence.

“Not all the time do we go fishing, sometimes we just plan to go out together for a fishing trip,” she said.

When conditions are favourable, the women often return home with malea (tilapia), duna (freshwater eels) and ketedromo.

On better days, they remain at the fishing grounds until the afternoon and catch the 4pm bus home.

“If we are getting a lot of fish, we would return home in the afternoon,” Lavenia said.

Sometimes, when the catch is poor at Wainikavika, the women walk to another fishing spot near an old bridge to try their luck.

item.title: BODY & MIND I Can a dying giant rise again?
item.description: In 1889, my great grandparents arrived In Fiji with their 9 years old son as part of the indenture system. After the immigration processing they found themselves in a place called Navua where they started their 5 years of indentured servitude planting sugar cane. The conditions, as we’ve all come to know, were more than challenging. They were harsh and unrelenting because in many ways they were st
item.content:encoded:
In 1889, my great grandparents arrived In Fiji with their 9 years old son as part of the indenture system.

After the immigration processing they found themselves in a place called Navua where they started their 5 years of indentured servitude planting sugar cane. The conditions, as we’ve all come to know, were more than challenging. They were harsh and unrelenting because in many ways they were starting from scratch.

It was a time when sugar was slowly starting to become the lifeblood of Fiji. The sweet smell of its potential and success were in the air.

The rhythmic sound of cane trains crossing the countryside, the sight of farmers tending their crops beneath the tropical sun and the towering chimneys of the mills were all symbols of an industry that helping to forge a new frontier and to build a small island nation.

For generations, sugar put food on family tables. It paid school fees, built homes, sustained rural communities, and contributed significantly to Fiji’s economy. For decades it was Fiji’s number one export earner.

Today, however, that giant appears to be struggling and teetering on the brink of collapse. Without government subsidies it would most certainly collapse.

Cane production has declined. Many farmers have left the industry. Labour shortages continue to grow. Young people are increasingly seeking opportunities elsewhere and aging infrastructure has made it difficult for the industry to compete in a rapidly changing world.

The questions many are asking is simple:

Can Fiji’s sugar industry giant be revived and survive?

Can Fiji’s sugar industry reset itself, even reinvent itself?

History teaches us that industries rarely survive by standing still. They survive by evolving and adapting.

The challenges facing Fiji may not be a lack of opportunity but a lack of vision and imagination.

For decades, we’ve viewed sugar cane primarily as a source of sugar. Yet around the world, sugar cane has evolved into something much bigger.

In countries like Brazil, sugar cane helps power vehicles through ethanol fuel. Elsewhere, it’s used to generate electricity, manufacture industrial products, produce bio-plastics, create animal feed and support entire renewable energy sectors.

What if Fiji stopped seeing sugar cane simply as sugar?

What if we began viewing it as a renewable resource capable of generating multiple streams of income?

Imagine mills producing not only sugar but electricity. Imagine ethanol plants reducing dependence on imported fuel. Imagine waste products becoming valuable commodities rather than discarded by-products.

The future of sugar may not lie in sugar alone.

Of course, diversification by itself will not solve every problem.

The industry also faces another challenge that’s becoming increasingly apparent: its aging farming workforce.

Many of Fiji’s cane farmers have spent a lifetime on the land. Generations to be exact. Their knowledge and experience are invaluable. Yet younger generations are often reluctant to follow in their footsteps.

My paternal grandparents were the last in a line of indentured labourers in our family. They sent their children to study abroad. Their children became some of Fiji’s first professionals – a doctor, lawyer, two school teachers and a Methodist Minister.

Few young people are attracted to an industry that appears uncertain and offers limited financial rewards.

If Fiji wants young people to embrace agriculture, farming must become profitable, modern and technologically advanced.

Young minds are naturally drawn to opportunity. They’re innovators, problem-solvers and entrepreneurs. Give them an industry with a future and many will eagerly help shape it.

Mechanisation, improved training, access to finance and modern farming techniques could all play a role in making agriculture attractive once again.

Perhaps there’s another lesson hidden within the sugar industry’s struggles.

For years, discussions have focused on saving what once was a giant.

But the future rarely belongs to those who cling to yesterday.

It belongs to those willing to build tomorrow.

item.title: ACCOMPLISHMENT I Lal finds his purpose in hatchery work
item.description: AFTER graduating with a Bachelor of Science in Fisheries, Adrian Lal found himself in a position familiar to many young graduates, qualified, hopeful, but unable to immediately secure work in his chosen field. Instead of giving up, he took whatever work he could find.
item.content:encoded:
AFTER graduating with a Bachelor of Science in Fisheries, Adrian Lal found himself in a position familiar to many young graduates, qualified, hopeful, but unable to immediately secure work in his chosen field.

Instead of giving up, he took whatever work he could find.

He worked security jobs and did odd work to make ends meet while continuing to search for an opportunity that matched the years he had invested in his education.

“I had to find a job that was worth the school fees, so I tried hard to secure it,” he said.

Today, the young man from Waitovu in Levuka, Ovalau, whose mother is from Savusavu, works as a hatchery technician at Growa Fish Limited in Navua, where he has spent the past three years helping breed and raise fish and freshwater prawns that support Fiji’s growing aquaculture industry.

It is a career that not only gives him purpose but also allows him to continue learning every day.

Adrian lives on-site at the hatchery, making it easier to respond to the demands of the job.

Every morning begins with a routine that leaves little room for error.

As soon as he enters the hatchery, he inspects each Larval Rearing Tank (LRT), ensuring the tiny prawns have enough oxygen and feed while checking for any mortality or other issues.

“Temperature plays a vital role in the larvae culture of prawns,” he said.

“The prawns change into different stages due to certain temperatures, which is around 28 to 30 degrees Celsius. They are able to adapt, feed and change well in that kind of temperature.”

Once the temperature is checked, Adrian measures the ammonia levels in the water.

“If the ammonia is high, I have to do a water exchange. If it’s low, then it’s okay.”

He also monitors dissolved oxygen levels before ensuring sufficient feed is available.

“We are feeding our larvae with artemia, which is live feed, and then I update my record sheet.”

Feeding does not stop after the morning checks.

He monitors feeding every two hours throughout the day to ensure the larvae continue developing under the right conditions.

His responsibilities extend beyond the hatchery tanks.

He assists customers purchasing tilapia, helps with general farm operations and is currently involved in breeding goldfish while also working with grass carp, which are used to control weed growth inside and around ponds.

“The day here goes really fast,” Adrian said.

“Sometimes we can still be doing something and then you realise it’s already 6pm.”

Although Adrian now works in aquaculture, he admits it was not always the career path he imagined.

“I had thought of a lot of things for my career path, but since I did my bachelor’s in science and Fisheries at Koronivia, I had to look for a job and I happened to come across the advertisement here, so I gave it a try.”

That decision proved life changing for Adrian.

Working under the guidance of Growa Fish Limited owner Avinash Singh has expanded both his practical skills and his understanding of aquaculture.

“Here I’m learning something new every day,” he said.

What started as employment has gradually become something much deeper.

Long before Adrian entered a hatchery, his connection to aquatic life began at home.

Growing up beside the sea sparked a fascination that has remained with him into adulthood.

“I lived beside the sea growing up and I loved it, so coming here became a passion and interest.”

item.title: BLAST FROM THE PART I How one musket changed Fiji forever
item.description: He was known to our ancestors by a Fijian version of his Swedish name, Jale Seveti. However, Charles Savage, born Kalle Svenson, would leave a mark on these islands far greater than any chief or priest before him.
item.content:encoded:
He was known to our ancestors by a Fijian version of his Swedish name, Jale Seveti.

However, Charles Savage, born Kalle Svenson, would leave a mark on these islands far greater than any chief or priest before him.

The colourful story of his exploits tells us not just about his drive for power and influence but also, more importantly, how his skill with firearms recalibrated the centuries-old political order in Fiji.

Savage arrived in these islands not by design but by disaster.

His vessel, the 135-tonne American brig Eliza, had foundered on the reef at Nairai at the opening of the nineteenth century.

Marooned and resourceful, the red-haired Swede did not despair.

He had already spent time in Tonga, where he had absorbed knowledge of Fijian society from the mataisau, or carpenters, of Kabara, and he wasted little of it.

At Nairai, he was taught the Fijian language by a local chief named Tui Lawaki, and through him began to acquire the cultural intelligence that would eventually make him indispensable to one of the most powerful rulers in the archipelago.

It was at Nairai, too, that Savage encountered the network of chiefly politics that would determine his fate.

Chiefs associated with Levuka and Bau were passing through, travelling from Lakeba and Koro to attend ceremonies confirming the Vunivalu of Bau as Tui Levuka.

Drawn to this world rather than repelled by it, Savage chose to join the party. Accompanied by a Chinese cook named Lui, they journeyed to Bau under the leadership of Daulakeba, hereditary chief of the Levuka people of Lakeba who trace their lineage back to Bau.

Ratu Naulivou Radomodomo, Vunivalu of Bau, was a man of fierce and calculating intelligence.

His island chieftaincy sat at the edge of a vast river delta, its canoes commanding the waters that connected the interior of Viti Levu to the open sea.

Bau was powerful, but its ambitions were perpetually frustrated by rivals, among them being Nakelo, a fortified settlement commanding a stretch of river that Bau’s canoes could not legally use.

The prohibition had been imposed by a bete, or priest, of Nakelo, and it had held for years.

When the newcomer Savage was brought before him, Naulivou’s first question was regarding firearms.

Savage explained that weapons of this kind could sometimes be salvaged from wrecks.

Daulakeba confirmed that a vessel carrying such arms lay wrecked in the Lau group.

The Vunivalu immediately ordered both men to sail there at once and return with whatever could be recovered, and this they did.

Beckoned on by favourable winds, they reached Oneata, where Savage boarded the wreck himself and recovered muskets, gunpowder and ammunition.

The firearms were brought back to Bau, where Savage gave a demonstration of the musket’s effect and the Vunivalu, greatly impressed, resolved at once to use the new weapon in battle.

Bau’s war party set out, and when the two sides faced each other across the fortifications, Savage was positioned on an elevated platform above the battlefield — a marksman with a commanding view of the field below.

What followed altered the course of Fijian history in a matter of seconds.

At once, the leading warrior of Nakelo collapsed, dead before he hit the ground. No spear had touched him. No club had found him. He had simply fallen.

The warriors of Nakelo could not comprehend what they were witnessing. Their trenches were unbreached. Their sharpened defensive stakes still stood. Yet men were dying inside their own fortifications. To those defending the settlement, the only explanation was one of dread, for it seemed that Bau had brought with it a new and terrible god.

Panic spread with extraordinary speed. Warriors abandoned their positions and fled in all directions. When they broke from the outer defences, they ran straight into Bau’s reserve forces, who had been waiting precisely for such a moment. The confusion became total. The defenders fought while consumed by terror of an unseen power; the attackers advanced in the full knowledge of what that power was.

As the two sides became entangled in close combat, Savage ceased firing. He could no longer distinguish friend from enemy and feared killing Bau’s own men. The warriors of Bau poured into the village, preventing women and children from escaping. Then they set it alight. The houses stood close together and the flames consumed them rapidly.

When the fighting ended, Savage climbed down from his platform and took in the aftermath. Preparations for cannibal feasting were already under way, and it was the first such scene he had witnessed with his own eyes.

Deeply disturbed, he resolved to speak to the Vunivalu directly. But as he approached, something stopped him.

A small procession was emerging from the direction of Nakelo and moving slowly along the riverbank. It was unlike any ordinary ceremony. The men who led it carried baskets filled with soil. Bau’s warriors parted to let them pass. The noise of victory fell away into silence.

item.title: YOUTH I Acts of kindness stop Lami traffic
item.description: ON a quiet roadside within the Lami Town boundary, young people are discovering that sometimes the simplest acts—a healthy breakfast, a friendly greeting and an open conversation—can strengthen a community more than any grand programme. On Saturday mornings, between Nadonumai Rd and Suvavou Village in (Vugalei, Lami), looks much like any other.
item.content:encoded:
ON a quiet roadside within the Lami Town boundary, young people are discovering that sometimes the simplest acts—a healthy breakfast, a friendly greeting and an open conversation—can strengthen a community more than any grand programme.

On Saturday mornings, between Nadonumai Rd and Suvavou Village in (Vugalei, Lami), looks much like any other.

Vehicles pass steadily. Workers head to their jobs. Some are rushing to appointments, others simply trying to get through another busy day.

But tucked into a quiet corner beside the roadside, another routine is unfolding.

Youth volunteers from the Seventh-day Adventist Church are already at work, arranging tables, pouring freshly prepared health juices and serving wholesome breakfasts to anyone who stops.

There is no registration desk. No speeches. No expectation that visitors attend church or sit through a sermon. Just breakfast, a simple conversation and people making time for other people.

Week after week, what began as a simple act of service has become a space where relationships are formed, strangers become familiar faces and a sense of community is rebuilt.

For deputy youth leader, Sakiasi Tuni, the weekly programme is never simply about giving away food.

“The main purpose is to serve the community through practical acts of kindness while promoting healthy living and building meaningful relationships,” he says.

“Our target audience includes everyone in the Vugalei community and surrounding areas—drivers, workers, families and residents.”

“We want people to understand that the church cares not only about their spiritual needs but also about their physical and mental well-being.”

It is a philosophy that feels remarkably relevant in today’s Fiji. Communities continue to wrestle with growing social challenges—from substance abuse and the devastating impact of methamphetamine to NCDs and increasing concerns around mental health.

While such issues rarely have simple solutions, initiatives like this offer genuine human connection.

A warm breakfast cannot solve every problem. But according to Tuni, it can open a conversation.

The volunteers avoid making their roadside outreach feel like a religious campaign. Instead, they focus first on creating an environment where people feel welcome, respected and heard.

Tuni believes this approach follows the example of Jesus Christ, who first connected with people before asking anything of them.

“People are more receptive to the gospel when they see it lived out in everyday actions,” he explains.

“Building trust and genuine relationships demonstrates Christ’s love in a practical way.

“Jesus mingled with people, met their needs, and through. His actions won their confidence. We follow this model, often referred to as ‘Christ’s method alone’.

“We believe these meaningful acts create opportunities for conversations not only about physical needs but, most importantly, about faith. We have witnessed this happening time and time again.”

Rather than relying on formal programmes or structured outreach, the volunteers allow conversations to happen naturally.

Some visitors stop only long enough for a quick drink before continuing their journey.

Others linger. Many return. Over time, familiar faces become friendships.

Stories about young people often focus on the challenges they face.

Less attention is given to those volunteering before sunrise, using their time to serve complete strangers.

For the Vugalei SDA volunteers, community service has become part of their faith journey.

“Youth are not just the future of the church,” Tuni says.

“They are its present. Being active helps them develop leadership skills, a spirit of service and a sense of responsibility.

“It allows them to make a positive impact in their communities while growing in their faith.”

Their work reflects a broader shift taking place among many young Christians today.

item.title: DISCOVERING FIJI I A dawn that changed Fiji
item.description: ON the morning of October 12, 1835, two missionaries stood in a small boat approaching the shores of Lakeba island in Lau. Ahead of them, nearly 200-armed men gathered on the beach, some carried muskets, others held clubs, spears, bows and arrows.
item.content:encoded:
ON the morning of October 12, 1835, two missionaries stood in a small boat approaching the shores of Lakeba island in Lau.

Ahead of them, nearly 200-armed men gathered on the beach, some carried muskets, others held clubs, spears, bows and arrows.

Their faces painted black and red, many had never seen a European before.

The newcomers were Reverend William Cross, an Englishman, and Reverend David Cargill, a Scotsman and graduate of the University of Aberdeen.

They had travelled from Tonga carrying a mission that would alter the course of Fiji’s history.

One hundred years later, on the same date and at the same place, thousands would gather to commemorate their arrival.

Churches would be dedicated, descendants honoured and thanksgiving services held across Fiji.

What began with two missionaries seeking permission to stay on a remote island had become one of the most significant turning points in the nation’s story.

Cross and Cargill did not arrive in Fiji easily.

According to the Methodist Missionary Society’s preparations for the centenary, both men undertook missionary work throughout Tonga before setting their sights on Fiji.

They reached Vavau in March 1835 intending to cross immediately, but circumstances delayed their plans. For six months they waited.

Only when the schooner Blackbird arrived were they able to continue their journey.

On October 8, 1835, they sailed from Vavau and four days later, on October 12, Lakeba came into view.

The date would later become one of the most important anniversaries in Fiji’s religious history.

David Cargill’s diary provides one of the most vivid first-hand accounts of the encounter.

As the island emerged from the sea on the morning of October 12, uncertainty hung over the expedition.

The captain possessed no chart of the harbour and had never previously visited Lakeba.

Cargill recorded: “It was therefore determined that Mr Cross and I should go ashore in the boat and visit the king of the island.”

“As we approached the beach, we saw many of the natives running hither and thither on the sand,” Cargill wrote.

Soon they found themselves facing a large gathering of armed men.

“As we drew near to the landing place, nearly 200 men, some armed with muskets – others with bayonets fastened to long sticks, some with clubs and spears, others with bow and arrows were standing about 100 feet distant from the place at which we stepped on shore.”

“Sa loloma kivei kemundou” (my respects to you).

The crowd stepped back – the strangers were objects of curiosity and uncertainty.

“They were astonished and seemed not to know what to think of us,” Cargill wrote.

The moment captured a meeting of worlds, two missionaries carrying a foreign faith stood before people whose customs and beliefs were largely unknown to them.

The path from the beach led inland to the residence of Tui Nayau, the paramount chief of Lakeba.

According to Cargill, the chief initially waited in a house near the shore before returning to his main residence, located within a fortified settlement.

The missionary described the impressive defensive works surrounding the village.

“The settlement in which the king’s house stands, is surrounded by a stone wall, built without mortar; on top of the wall, a fence of reeds is erected, and the whole is surrounded by a moat nearly 100 feet wide,” he wrote.

Cross and Cargill brought gifts and explained the purpose of their visit.

item.title: LOCAL GETAWAYS | Stepping back in time
